words.shortDefinition: A concurrency design pattern that you can use on Android to simplify code that executes asynchronously.
words.longDefinition: Coroutines are like lightweight threads that help manage long-running tasks that might otherwise block the main thread. They allow for non-blocking asynchronous code that is as easy to read as synchronous code.
words.techContext: We use Kotlin coroutines to perform network requests and database operations without freezing the application's UI.
words.exampleSentence: Using a coroutine with the Dispatchers.IO scope ensured that the file download didn't block the user's interaction with the app.

words.shortDefinition: A single, focused thing that the user can do in an Android app.
words.longDefinition: Almost all activities interact with the user, so the Activity class takes care of creating a window for you in which you can place your UI. An app is usually a collection of multiple activities (e.g., LoginActivity, DashboardActivity).
words.techContext: The MainActivity is the entry point of our application, responsible for setting up the initial UI layout.
words.exampleSentence: The app crashed because I tried to start a new activity that hadn't been declared in the AndroidManifest.xml file.

words.shortDefinition: A messaging object you can use to request an action from another app component.
words.longDefinition: Intents allow you to start activities, services, or deliver broadcasts. They can be 'explicit' (targeting a specific class) or 'implicit' (asking the system to find an app that can handle a specific action like 'open a web page').
words.techContext: I created an implicit Intent with the ACTION_VIEW action to open the user's profile URL in their default web browser.
words.exampleSentence: We passed the user ID to the DetailActivity using an Intent extra.
words.etymology: from Latin intentus ('a stretching out, purpose').

codeExample.code:
val intent = Intent(this, TargetActivity::class.java)
intent.putExtra("KEY", "Value")
startActivity(intent)

words.shortDefinition: A class that is responsible for preparing and managing the data for an Activity or a Fragment.
words.longDefinition: Part of the Android Architecture Components. A ViewModel is designed to store and manage UI-related data in a lifecycle-conscious way, allowing data to survive configuration changes such as screen rotations.
words.techContext: By moving the network call logic into the ViewModel, we ensured that the data isn't re-fetched when the user rotates their phone.
words.exampleSentence: The ViewModel communicates with the repository to get the latest data and exposes it to the View via LiveData or Flow.

words.shortDefinition: Android's modern toolkit for building native UI.
words.longDefinition: A declarative UI framework that simplifies and accelerates UI development on Android. It allows developers to define UI using Kotlin functions instead of XML layouts, similar to React or SwiftUI.
words.techContext: We refactored our settings screen using Jetpack Compose, which reduced our code by 50% compared to the old XML-based approach.
words.exampleSentence: With Jetpack Compose, the UI state is automatically updated whenever the underlying data model changes.

words.shortDefinition: An observable data holder class that is lifecycle-aware.
words.longDefinition: Unlike a regular observable, LiveData respects the lifecycle of other app components (like activities). This means it only updates observers that are in an active state (Started or Resumed), preventing crashes and memory leaks.
words.techContext: Our ViewModel uses LiveData to provide the current weather information to the main activity.
words.exampleSentence: The activity automatically stopped receiving LiveData updates when it went into the background, saving system resources.

words.shortDefinition: A cold asynchronous data stream that sequentially emits values.
words.longDefinition: Built on top of coroutines, Flow is a more powerful and flexible alternative to LiveData. It supports complex operators (like map, filter, zip) and can handle multiple values being emitted over time.
words.techContext: We used a StateFlow to represent the state of our UI, ensuring that the last emitted value is always available to new collectors.
words.exampleSentence: The repository returns a Flow of search results that we collect in the ViewModel using the 'collect' function.

words.shortDefinition: An abstraction layer over SQLite that allows for more robust database access.
words.longDefinition: Part of the Jetpack suite. Room provides an object-mapping layer that allows for fluent database access while harnessing the full power of SQLite. It provides compile-time verification of SQL queries.
words.techContext: We used Room to cache the user's offline messages so they can still read them without an internet connection.
words.exampleSentence: Room's @Dao annotation made it easy to define our database queries using simple Kotlin interfaces.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a 'room' (space) to store things.

words.shortDefinition: A dependency injection library for Android built on top of Dagger.
words.longDefinition: Hilt simplifies Dagger-related tasks by providing a standard way to incorporate Dagger dependency injection into an Android application. It automatically handles the lifecycle of the injected objects.
words.techContext: We used Hilt to inject our networking and database dependencies into our ViewModels, making the code much easier to test.
words.exampleSentence: Adding the @HiltAndroidApp annotation to our application class was the first step in setting up dependency injection.
words.etymology: from 'hilt' (the handle of a sword/tool), implying something that makes Dagger easier to hold.

words.shortDefinition: A modular portion of an activity's user interface.
words.longDefinition: A fragment has its own lifecycle and receives its own input events. You can add or remove fragments while the activity is running. Fragments are useful for creating multi-pane UIs and reusable UI components.
words.techContext: We used a Fragment for the 'Profile' section so it could be reused in both the bottom navigation and a side drawer.
words.exampleSentence: The fragment's view was destroyed, but the fragment instance remained in memory until the activity was finished.
words.etymology: from Latin fragmentum ('a piece broken off').

words.shortDefinition: A required file that describes essential information about an app to the Android build tools and OS.
words.longDefinition: AndroidManifest.xml. It declares the app's components (activities, services), permissions (internet, camera), and hardware requirements. The OS uses this file to understand what the app can do and how it should be launched.
words.techContext: I had to add the INTERNET permission to the manifest so our app could fetch the user's feed from our server.
words.exampleSentence: Every activity must be declared in the manifest, or the system won't be able to start it.
words.etymology: from Latin manifestus ('evident, caught in the act'), meaning a list of contents.

words.shortDefinition: An app component that can perform long-running operations in the background.
words.longDefinition: Unlike an activity, a service does not provide a user interface. A service can continue to run even if the user switches to another app. Examples include playing music in the background or downloading data over the network.
words.techContext: We used a Foreground Service to ensure that the music player doesn't get killed by the OS while the user is using other apps.
words.exampleSentence: The background service was responsible for periodically syncing the local database with the server.
words.etymology: from Latin servitium ('condition of a slave/servant').

words.shortDefinition: A component that allows your app to respond to system-wide broadcast announcements.
words.longDefinition: The system sends broadcasts when events occur, such as the battery being low or the screen turning off. Apps can also initiate broadcasts. A receiver acts as a gateway for these events, even when the app isn't running.
words.techContext: We registered a broadcast receiver to listen for the 'BOOT_COMPLETED' event so our app can reschedule its background alarms.
words.exampleSentence: The broadcast receiver triggered a notification as soon as the device connected to a charger.

words.shortDefinition: The build automation system used for Android apps.
words.longDefinition: Handles compiling the code, managing dependencies, and packaging the app into an APK or AAB. Developers configure the build process using 'build.gradle' files (written in Groovy or Kotlin DSL).
words.techContext: I updated the dependencies in the Gradle file to include the latest version of the Firebase SDK.
words.exampleSentence: The Gradle build failed because of a version conflict between two different libraries we were using.

words.shortDefinition: Android Runtime; the managed runtime used by applications and some system services.
words.longDefinition: Replaced the older Dalvik VM. ART uses AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation to improve app performance by compiling bytecode into native machine code when the app is installed, rather than during execution.
words.techContext: Because of ART's AOT compilation, Android apps launch faster and use less CPU during execution compared to the old Dalvik runtime.
words.exampleSentence: The new version of the Android OS included significant optimizations to ART, improving battery life across all devices.

words.shortDefinition: Android App Bundle (AAB); a publishing format that includes all your app's compiled code and resources.
words.longDefinition: Unlike an APK, an AAB cannot be installed directly. When you upload it to Google Play, the store generates optimized APKs for each user's specific device configuration (language, screen density, CPU architecture).
words.techContext: Switching to Android App Bundles reduced our app's average download size by 25%.
words.exampleSentence: Google Play now requires all new apps to be uploaded as App Bundles instead of APKs.

words.shortDefinition: A tool that shrinks, optimizes, and obfuscates your Java and Kotlin code.
words.longDefinition: It removes unused code and renames classes and methods with short, meaningless names (e.g., 'a', 'b'). This makes the app smaller and much harder to reverse-engineer. In modern Android, this is handled by R8.
words.techContext: We enabled ProGuard in our production build to prevent competitors from easily decompiling our proprietary algorithms.
words.exampleSentence: The app crashed in production because ProGuard had accidentally removed a class that was being accessed via reflection.

words.shortDefinition: A type-safe HTTP client for Android and Java.
words.longDefinition: The industry standard for making network requests in Android apps. It allows you to define your API endpoints as simple Kotlin interfaces and handles the conversion of JSON responses into data objects automatically.
words.techContext: We used Retrofit with Moshi to parse the JSON responses from our backend into our domain models.
words.exampleSentence: Retrofit makes our networking layer extremely clean by abstracting away the low-level HTTP details.
words.etymology: from 'retro-' (backwards) + 'fit', implying an addition of new parts to something old.

words.shortDefinition: A library for managing persistent, deferrable background tasks.
words.longDefinition: Part of Jetpack. It handles background work that needs to run even if the app exits or the device restarts. It automatically chooses the best way to run the task based on the OS version (e.g., using JobScheduler or AlarmManager).
words.techContext: We used WorkManager to schedule a daily backup of the local database to the cloud.
words.exampleSentence: WorkManager ensures that our image upload task eventually succeeds, even if the network is flaky or the phone restarts.

words.shortDefinition: A technology that allows you to share code across different platforms (Android, iOS, Web, Desktop).
words.longDefinition: Abbreviated as KMP. Unlike Flutter, KMP focuses on sharing business logic, networking, and data validation while allowing you to build the UI using native frameworks like SwiftUI and Jetpack Compose.
words.techContext: By using KMP, we were able to write our data sync logic once and use it in both our Android and iOS applications.
words.exampleSentence: Kotlin Multiplatform allowed us to maintain a single 'source of truth' for our complex business rules.

words.shortDefinition: The sequence of states an Android component goes through.
words.longDefinition: For an Activity, this includes states like Created, Started, Resumed, Paused, Stopped, and Destroyed. Developers use lifecycle callbacks (like onCreate or onResume) to manage resources and UI updates.
words.techContext: I added a cleanup task in the onStop() callback to release the camera resources when the user switches to another app.
words.exampleSentence: Understanding the activity lifecycle is essential for preventing memory leaks and ensuring a smooth user experience.

words.shortDefinition: A framework for navigating between different screens in an Android app.
words.longDefinition: Simplifies the implementation of navigation by providing a central navigation graph (XML), automated handling of Fragment transactions, and standard patterns for the back button and drawers.
words.techContext: We used the Navigation Component to define all the possible paths through our multi-step onboarding flow.
words.exampleSentence: The Navigation component handled the deep link from the notification and took the user directly to the specific chat screen.

words.shortDefinition: A technique that binds UI components in layouts to data sources in your app.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to declare UI components in your XML layouts that directly reference your data models. This reduces the 'boilerplate' code needed to manually update views (like findViewByID or setText).
words.techContext: With data binding, we linked the User object directly to the XML layout, so the name field updates automatically when the object changes.
words.exampleSentence: Data binding helped us eliminate hundreds of lines of code that were just copying data from the ViewModel to the UI views.

words.shortDefinition: A component that manages access to a central repository of data.
words.longDefinition: Allows an app to share data with other apps (like the contacts list or photo gallery). It provides a standard interface for querying and manipulating data across different application processes.
words.techContext: Our app uses a Content Provider to allow the system's search widget to index our application's local documents.
words.exampleSentence: We had to implement a custom Content Provider to securely share the user's exported reports with the file manager app.

words.shortDefinition: Android Package Kit; the file format used by the Android OS for distribution and installation of mobile apps.
words.longDefinition: A ZIP-like file that contains the app's code (DEX files), resources, assets, and the manifest. While AAB is used for publishing to Google Play, the APK is what actually gets installed on the device.
words.techContext: I sent the debug APK to the testing team so they could side-load it on their test devices.
words.exampleSentence: The APK size was too large, so we removed several unused high-resolution images to stay under the 100MB limit.
